chunks: "It’s true," she gasps, looking up from the floor, her eyes filling with tears that are half real, half performance. "Dad, look at it—it’s Photoshopped. That’s not our room. That’s not... that’s not his..." She gestures wildly at Ethan, at his body, her mind racing. "Ethan was upset because Jason showed him this horrible site, and I was trying to calm him down, and I had a boy over earlier, I did, that’s why the room is messy, that’s why I have these marks, I’m sorry, I’m so sorry, I had a boy over and Ethan caught us and he was upset and that’s why he’s sweating and weird, because we were fighting about it—"  The lie hangs in the air, ragged and desperate, but she sees her mother’s expression shift, the horror fracturing into a different shape—disappointment, anger, but the anger of a mother who thinks her daughter is sneaking boys in, not the annihilating rage of discovering incest. "You had a boy in my house?" her mother whispers, clutching her chest, and Maya nods frantically, sobbing now in earnest, the fear and adrenaline pouring out of her in heaving waves. "Yes, yes, I’m sorry, his name is... is Mark, from the youth group, and we were just kissing, I swear, and Ethan came home early and saw us and he was so upset, and that’s why he’s been acting weird, and the photo is fake, Dad, please, look at the pixels or whatever, it’s not real, someone is pretending to be us—"  Her father stares at the photo, his hand trembling, and for a moment Maya thinks he will see through it, will recognize the angle of her jaw, the specific shape of Ethan’s hip, the wedding photo visible in the background of the original frame. But he is a man who sees what he expects to see, and he expects to see the corruption of the internet, the pollution of his children by the outside world, not the corruption that has bloomed between them in his own home. "If this is a prank," he says slowly, his voice dangerous, "if this is some internet garbage, I will have the police involved.

I will have every boy in that youth group questioned. I will—"  "It’s not a prank," Maya sobs, crawling forward to grasp his pant leg, pressing her forehead against his knee in a parody of repentance. "It’s a mistake. I’m sorry. I’ll never see him again. Please don’t look in my room. Please don’t look in the drawer."  It is the wrong thing to say. Her father’s eyes narrow, sharp and suspicious, and he turns back toward the stairs, the photograph clutched in his fist. "The drawer?" he repeats. Maya’s blood turns to ice. She has just marked the exact location of the phone, the crypto wallet, the evidence of their true sin. She opens her mouth to correct herself, to say she meant the closet, the bed, anywhere else, but her father is already mounting the stairs two at a time, driven now by a new suspicion, a new target for his wrath.
